result:
The first thing that seams, always, fresh in memory is the sound of freezing rain on glass—like dozens of tiny castanets being shook by cold fingers inside the house wall itself. When I was ten my parents moved the family into a repurposed rectory at the lip of a hollow in the Appalachian foothills. The place was three stories of tilt: tilting toward the creek behind it, tilting away from the road, tilting in its cracked plaster cornices, tilting in the moods it put on its occupants at night. We had come from a tract-brick suburb beside a tire store, so the sudden expanse of fog and holler, of velvet-dark mornings that never seemed to end, was a geography that colonised my interior before I understood what colonisation meant.

Of the thirty-odd windows in the rectory, twenty-two did not open. Their paint fused them to the sill like scar tissue. Because we were poor, we made a game of making poverty look intentional: no heat upstairs beyond one kerosene heater balanced on a slab of marble stolen from a cemetery dumpster; blankets nailed across door frames to trap the warm; Sears catalogs ripped into spirals and rolled into “logs” for the fireplace. At dusk the ice rain would arrive, and because I was the oldest, I was sent each evening to the side porch to retrieve more of the scrapwood my father collected from demolished barns. The porch roof leaked in such a reliable pattern that icicles grew down like root systems. I would need to snap one off, bring it into the living room, and let it steam in an enamel basin, pretending the meltwater was going to become soup. There was one electric light in that entire floor, a single bulb dangling from a wire the colour of rusted rose stems. The bulb had a pull chain enamelled with tiny hula dancers. I still believe that dancing chain was the warmest thing we owned; it seemed to sway even when the house was motionless, kept alive by some electricity of memory inside itself.

For years I thought everyone inhabited landscape the way we did: negotiating with it, like it had moods to be appeased. We made jokes about the ghosts of the priests in the hallway, but the bigger presences were subtler: the sour mash smell coming up through the floorboards when the creek flooded, the grey film of mildew that lived in my sister’s lungs and required Big Pharm’s antibiotics delivered every winter by the one mailman whose sedan could manage our gravel. At school no one believed a creek could flood upward; they pictured water spilling neatly across a flat bed as in the textbooks. I learned early the uselessness of fact against someone who has never seen the water itself climb the walls.

Memory, I now realise, is less a library and more a set of drafting tables. Engineers sit at each, redrawing what already happened, annotating, adding reinforcements. When I summon the rectory, newer details—things from decades later—arrive perched on the rafters. The frescoes my parents never painted up there suddenly appear, fresher than original memory. Overwriting adds texture but also costs; I sometimes lose the exact way my mother’s voice slipped between registers when she counted pennies for the electric bill. It’s as if every subsequent year puts another pane of frosted glass between the event and me, and only when I tilt my head at the right angle can the original colours refract a true beam through.

We left that house the spring I turned fourteen, when my father’s unemployment ran out and the Diocese wanted the property back for a retreat center that never quite materialised. The last night, my siblings and I walked room to empty room. We had rolled up our sleeping bags; the furniture was not ours; the walls were suddenly enormous, cavernous. On the third floor I pressed my ear to the spot where an old iron radiator had been torn out. Somewhere inside the plaster I swear I could hear that hula-dancer bulb swinging, though it had been removed days earlier. I whispered goodbye in three languages I picked up from library linguaphone cassettes: adiós, go n-éirí an bóthar leat, zài jiàn. A kind of insurance that one of them would still apply when the house changed tongues. Then we drove away in a Chevette the colour of dried lilac, every pothole shaking loose the undercoating like dandruff.

After that we ricocheted through a blur of marginal housing: a single-wide where the carpet smelled of wet spaniel; a month in a motel with weekly cockroach races on the bathroom linoleum; a mold-addled farmhouse lent by a cousin who collected Civil War bullets in peanut butter jars. Each move erased the last house a little more. Somewhere the rectory’s wallpaper peeled away from my mind; somewhere its smell of creosote and kerosene got replaced with the immediate mold of the farmhouse or the insecticide of the motel. But that moment of ice rain on glass remains in 3D, as if the present moment were a postcard slot allowing that precise sensory quadrant to tumble back into the room. I’ve lived now in four countries and fourteen domiciles total, and none of them manage to subdue the original country of weather.

When I was in graduate school—first time around—I took a course on memory and architecture. The professor asked us to draw a floor plan of a childhood home from memory. I stayed up until four a.m. mapping the rectory on tracing paper, only to discover the next morning that I had assigned second-floor closets to the first floor,and made five windows where there had ever been four. My spouse, then simply my lab partner in library obsession, pointed out gently that rooms had re-arranged themselves like obedient dogs. She laughed: “Houses don’t relocate their closets without permission.” But in dreams they do. In dreams those doors open onto staircases that lead nowhere and basements that have forgotten they are basements.

During a period when my twenties were turning themselves into thirties, I used to walk Manhattan in the hour just before dawn, when the sodium streetlights puddle gold onto empty Broadway. The city folds in on itself at that hour: steam arises from grates like spirit-coats shaking off sleep. I would trace a grid intending to end at the river, and every block evoked some shard of earlier life. A funeral parlour whose awning colour matched the kerosene stove; a synagogue whose side door latch clicked exactly like the sound my father made locking the Chevette (always twice, always a prayer under his breath against the alternator seizing). One night I walked as far as the Williamsburg Bridge approach and stood looking into the East River’s slaty surface. The water moved like a slow heartbeat, and I became aware, suddenly, of the rectory’s creek running parallel underneath, as if east and Appalachia were sewn by aquifers and faultlines I would never consent to survey.

I used to think accretion was adding layers; now I think it’s peeling back varnish laid on too thick by later interpretation. Example: I believed my mother possessed infinite patience because she could sit for four hours stringing buttons into chains to sell at flea markets, pennies toward shoes. But peeling a layer, I see her patience was a mask: fear of motion in case movement spilled the last order she had over chaos. Still another layer beneath: she liked the gleam of coloured plastic disks against her fingers, the small rainbows of commerce. Dig again and another thing—the buttons were leftovers from parishioners’ donations, and with each she whispered a name: Mrs. Shanley who drank peach schnapps, Mr. Holloway who died of black lung. Threads of elegy braided through nickel-and-dime pragmatism.

Everything loops back eventually. I ended up studying hydrogeology in part because water, apparently, refuses to adhere to property maps. A creek born on one mountaintop will seep under fences and voting districts, arrive in another county reeking of cow trough, appear later as city tap water carrying the last century’s mercury. Mapping groundwater requires learning what wants to stay invisible. I liked the humility of the science: draw cross-sections of invisible lakes, guess their depth by how cottonwoods lean. In one field season we camped for weeks beside an artesian well that had been flowing since the ’30s. The basin nearby collected abandoned refrigerators arranged like Stonehenge by teenagers. Every morning condensation stippled the metal doors so profusely they seemed to weep. My colleagues saw waste; I saw an accidental iconostasis: chrome doors thrown open to reveal reliquaries of bologna gone luminous with age.

How does any of this cohere—childhood, water, warped rooms, cost-of-living arithmetic? Only recently did I realise coherence is not necessary; resonance suffices. A thing resonates when plucked by an adjacent thing. Late last year I returned, for the first time, to the county where the rectory stands. Google maps suggested the building had been razed, replaced by a low-slung latex-painted chapel and parking lot with space for eighty vehicles. I parked anyway amid the white lines, walked behind till I found the creek still threading through kudzu, wider now, angrier. On the bank lay broken limestone, no doubt bulldozed when they levelled the old foundation. I lifted one shard; inside a hollow smaller than my thumb, groundwater had left a miniature stalactite, drip by drip forming a tiny column as if in mimicry of Chartres. The stone caught afternoon light and became momentarily translucent, like a word waiting to be spoken.

Science says limestone forms under ancient seas; memory says it builds also from kitchen arguments at 2 a.m. when parents decide which bill to postpone. Place the two side by side and they ring against each other in the key of C-minor. Someone passing by would hear only a pebble clack, but inside the body that sound opens like a rusted hinge: creak—creek—holler—home.

During those Manhattan night walks I used to carry a small cassette recorder, interviewing lamppost shadows for a project I never finished. Once on Crosby Street I recorded a steam hiss that resembled old women sighing. Listening years later I noticed a faint undertrack: a violin from an open apartment, playing a scale in D major every ten measures, as if stitching the city’s riverine length together. That violin did not know it shared tape space with steam, which did not know it communed with my childhood creek, which did not suspect it would travel inside limestone up Route 52 in my pocket decades later. Yet tied together, they form a single phonograph of longing running on a loop audible only when heart rate slows below 55 bpm.

Funny detail: I cannot conjure my father’s face in full focus anymore. What arrives in dreams is the outline of his glasses reflecting moonlight, but in the center blur an indeterminate shade. Yet I can still feel his hands showing me how to change the Chevette’s alternator by flashlight, two fingertips stained with battery acid now overlapping inside my own older knuckles. Tactile memory outlasts photographic, the way a riverbed stone remains when the river reroutes.

Once I measured time by house moves, now by bodily decay and apocalypse newsfeeds. During pandemic lockdown I stayed in an attic apartment whose landlord broadcast instructional videos on how to breathe for free. When windows were sealed against viral air, the aromatic ghost of earlier tenants drifted upward: curry, cat litter, cedar balls. I started muttering original psalms into the vent—tiny offerings that maybe travelled two floors down and soothed someone chopping onions. Memory, then, is not a restoration but a relay: one sentence passed by voice to air, air to particle, particle to the frog living in the drain, frog to me again years on when I find a mummified leg on the sill thinking it’s a misplaced bookmark. Memento mori becomes literally momento mori.

Everyone ends up curating some ideal museum of the past to ward off present incoherence. The danger is believing the exhibit is the city rather than its scale model. I keep saying “I” but the pronoun is porous: some days the it houses five people, none of whom agree on whether the kettle should be steel silver or chipped enamel. On such days I walk the local rail trail where invasive bamboo creaks like a ship in breeze. Each creak a metronome trying to regulate disparate selves into a single migratory flock. If I stare long enough, wind animates the hollow stems so they bend toward each other, whisper, reassume vertical. Disaster interrupted and resumed, a gesture older than Latin taxonomy.

My mother, still alive but forgetting, telephones with increasing frequency to announce the current date. She sometimes adds fictions: the rectory is now an orphanage run by ex-goat farmers. I play along, expanding. I tell her yes, I saw goat bells wired to the eaves to mimic cathedral carillon. Each invention adds weight to the one truth left—that she can still make me laugh across long distance. Her laughter still splits between youthful cigarette husk and the newer tremolo of age. Electricity flickers in wall-wiring memory, sometimes too dim to spin the meter, sometimes bright enough to jolt the heart.

One final image to offer before wordcount intervenes: imagine a man on a red-eye train rattling toward an academic conference he no longer believes in. Seated across is a boy, maybe nine, clutching a glass jar half-filled with creek water and three tadpoles. Between them rides dusk and dawn simultaneously. Outside the window, telephone poles slap past like ruler edges counting silent measures. Inside, the boy whispers to the jar: “Don’t be afraid, it’s only going somewhere.” The man, pretending not to listen, remembers another whisper carried on freezing rain decades earlier: “Hold steady, the house will settle.” Everything rhymes. The ear is larger than history books claim.

When the train arrives in whatever city pretends to be the future, man and boy disembark and vanish—one toward grandparents’ arms, one toward another lecture hall. In the discarded jar one surviving tadpole grows hind legs overnight. Nothing stays still. Even memory is a tadpole becoming. We turn around and the creek is always already climbing the walls; the hula bulb has never stopped swinging; the limestone remembers ocean, silence, milk of incredible pressure, hymn of ice. And even at this late hour, the platelet of yesterday pulses at the wrist—steady, unfaithful, home.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
